title: Why do bug fixes lead to constant response calls (CRs)? A personal reflection on the SpecKit architecture design.
description: A record of encountering consecutive CRs while fixing bugs in SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI): reviewing implicit coupling in the system and discussing personal views on the ideal architecture design for future evolution.
content:
**TL;DR:** While fixing a bug causing command calls to fail in SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI), I encountered a barrage of response reviews (CRs) from Copilot. Upon review, I discovered this wasn't simply a code error, but rather an implicit coupling in the architecture caused by the lack of a "single source of truth." Although the current PR only addresses the inconsistencies, this experience has prompted me to consider the future evolution of a "unified domain core" architecture and share some personal design philosophies. Recently, I spent considerable time dealing with a seemingly minor issue: in SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI), users couldn't smoothly invoke commands and trigger a fixed, efficient workflow like with other agents. Initially, my assessment of this problem was quite simplistic: it should be a localized fix. Adjusting the naming, generation logic, and invocation methods, adding some tests, and that would be it. However, this turned out to be entirely different. During the fix process, Copilot Reviews continued to provide new CRs. One fix after another, another popped up; a seemingly minor change affecting only the local machine eventually unravels a whole chain of issues involving presets, extensions, hooks, release packaging, and Kimi compatibility migrations. The pressure was immense, and it was incredibly energy-consuming. Finally, the problem was resolved, and the PRs subsided. But the most valuable aspect of this period wasn't actually "fixing the bugs," but rather using the process to clearly see some real architectural problems within the codebase. This article aims to share the post-mortem analysis behind this fix. --- ## Starting Point: A Seemingly Small Problem The initial problem was very specific: The experience of invoking quick workflows via commands in the SpecKit terminal was incorrect. Users expected to quickly enter a standardized workflow through a skill-like approach; however, the existing implementation's naming, generation, and invocation methods weren't fully aligned, leading to inconsistent experiences, and in some cases, failing to correctly target the actual output. From a purely functional perspective, this seemed like a minor fix to naming or generation logic. But digging deeper revealed that the problem wasn't limited to a single feature. Because this capability is far more than just "a file generator." It's connected to: - Agent configuration - Command-to-skill mapping - Preset installation and uninstallation - Automatic extension registration - Hook invocation hints - GitHub release packaging script - Local`uv run specify`Differences from Remote Release Packages - Kimi's Historical Compatibility and Migration Logic In other words, you think you're fixing a button, but you're actually dealing with a fragmented protocol system. --- ## Why Copilot Keeps Generating CRs The most stressful part of this process isn't the difficulty of writing the code, but rather the constant stream of new CRs from Copilot. Initially, a natural reaction might be: Have I missed too many things? Is the review bot "smarter" than me? Later, my assessment changed: It's not that it's smarter, but rather that it considers the issue from the perspective of "consistency across the entire PR," while I initially focused more on "fixing issues within the current thread." These two perspectives are vastly different. What I did back then was essentially: - Fixing an issue pointed out in existing comments - Getting the current tests pass - Ensuring the behavior holds true in that specific area But Copilot was looking at something else entirely: - Is this change consistent with other module conventions? - Are production and consumption symmetrical? Are naming conventions consistent across all entry points? Is deletion, restoration, and migration a complete closed loop? Are local logic and release artifacts consistent? Because of this, many problems manifested like this: - The code itself looks correct The tests pass But when placed within the entire chain, it falters. What I truly felt this time was: **Many CRs aren't "implementation errors," but rather "system invariants aren't being expressed consistently."** --- ## The Root of Pressure Isn't Just a Lot of CRs To be honest, the pressure of this round of reviews was immense. Because it's not the kind of pressure where you "write an if statement incorrectly" and then it's over; it's more like: - You fix one problem - The review then points out another inconsistency from a different entry point You start to doubt whether your understanding of the overall picture is complete enough Every push might trigger a new thread This feeling is very different from simply fixing bugs. It's more like patching holes in the dark in a system without a clear center. Looking back, what really put pressure on me wasn't the number of comments themselves, but something it revealed: **I initially underestimated the discreteness of this business logic.** I thought I was fixing a feature, but in reality, I was dealing with a network of rules scattered across multiple modules, scripts, and artifact chains. --- ## Understanding the Current Skill Architecture with a Diagram The most impactful point for me this time was: The problem wasn't in a single function, but rather that the entire skill chain's "production-consumption-recovery-migration" was quite fragmented. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Producers["Skill 产物生产端"] InitPy["Python: install_ai_skills()"] CmdReg["Python: CommandRegistrar.render_skill_command()"] ExtReg["Python: ExtensionManager._register_extension_skills()"] PresetReg["Python: PresetManager._register_skills()"] ReleaseSH["Bash: create-release-packages.sh"] ReleasePS["PowerShell: create-release-packages.ps1"] end subgraph Artifact["核心产物"] SkillFile["//SKILL.md"] end subgraph Consumers["Skill 产物消费端"] Hook["Hook invocation / hook message"] ExtRemove["Extension remove / cleanup"] PresetRemove["Preset remove / restore"] Migration["Legacy migration"] end InitPy --> SkillFile CmdReg --> SkillFile ExtReg --> SkillFile PresetReg --> SkillFile ReleaseSH --> SkillFile ReleasePS --> SkillFile SkillFile --> Hook SkillFile --> ExtRemove SkillFile --> PresetRemove SkillFile --> Migration ```The most important aspect of this diagram isn't the number of lines, but rather the fact it illustrates: **The same`SKILL.md`There are many producers and many consumers. This means: - Changing only one production logic doesn't mean other production logics will change accordingly. - Changing only one consumption logic doesn't mean other restore/delete logics will understand the same rules. - Fixing the local Python doesn't mean the GitHub release package is correct. - Any synchronization issues between Kimi/Codex/extension/preset/hook will cause drift. This is why there are so many CRs (Confirmation Responses) in this PR. You think you're fixing a single point, but you're actually touching a net. --- ## Why a Small Change Triggers a Series of CRs If we abstract this problem into one sentence, it's actually: **A rule without a single source of truth.** For example, the "skill naming convention" isn't a unified interface in the current architecture; it's scattered across many places. ```mermaid flowchart LR Change["修改一条 skill 规则
例如：speckit.plan -> speckit-plan"] A["__init__.py
init / migration"] B["agents.py
command -> skill 渲染"] C["extensions.py
extension 注册 / hook"] D["presets.py
preset 覆盖 / restore"] E["release .sh
远程包生成"] F["release .ps1
远程包生成"] Change --> A Change --> B Change --> C Change --> D Change --> E Change --> F ```The intuitive feeling this structure brings is: - The scope of changes is much larger than it appears. - It's easy to miss changes. - Even if you miss one, the system may still appear to be running, but the review will quickly find it. If tests aren't written as "invariants," it's difficult to spot them all at once. Copilot kept giving CRs this time not because it was constantly "nitpicking," but because this type of architecture inherently exposes inconsistencies. --- ## Several Core Issues Exposed This Time ### 1. Skill is not a first-class object, but a set of string conventions. The most obvious problem this time is that the concept of skill was not modeled as a stable domain object. It's more like a set of conventions scattered throughout: - How to name directories - In frontmatter`name`How to write it: - How to render invocation when hooking suggestions - How to restore when preset is removed - How to generate when extension is installed - How to package in the release script Many of these rules are not expressed through a unified interface, but are implemented separately through string concatenation, path conventions, and YAML assembly. The result is: If you change the naming rules of skills, it will almost certainly affect the entire chain. ### 2. The producer and consumer are not from a single source I later specifically checked it again and finally understood the problem completely: - Python code generates skills locally - Shell/PowerShell release scripts also generate skills - By default`specify init`The priority is still to use the GitHub release package. What does this mean? It means that even if your local code is correct, it doesn't mean the user "gets the correct version by default." This is a particularly typical pitfall this time: Local`--offline`The generated content is correct, but the Kimi skill metadata in the remote release package is still outdated, resulting in inconsistencies in the user's default initialization. This kind of problem is very difficult to spot intuitively without a single source. ### 3. The Lifecycle Logic is Dispersed If a system is only responsible for "generating," many problems are not so obvious. However, skills are not just generated once; they involve a complete lifecycle: - Generation during init - Registration during extension installation - Overriding during preset installation - Prompt invocation during hook consumption - Restoration or deletion during remove - Migration for historical naming compatibility These actions are scattered across different files, cooperating with each other through implicit conventions. Without a unified model, problems like this easily occur: - The logic of create changes - Remove doesn't change accordingly Restore still interprets it according to the old rules Hook still suggests the old name This bug is very persistent because each function seems "logical" on its own, but the system's overall execution is incorrect. ### 4. Migration is just "moving directories," not "semantic migration." A very typical example of this is the migration of Kimi's historical skills. The directory names have been moved from the old format to the new format, but...`SKILL.md`Inside`frontmatter.name`No changes were made. This resulted in a very awkward situation: - The directory is new - The metadata is still old This type of problem indicates that the migration only performed a file system-level move, without standardizing the artifact semantics. This might be a workable compromise in the early stages, but as the system becomes more complex, it becomes a long-term liability. --- ## What did this fix ultimately solve? Although the process was cumbersome, it ultimately resolved the main issues. Including: - Alignment of skill naming between Codex and Kimi - Alignment of hook invocation with the actual skill directory - Automatic placeholder/path parsing completion for skill registration in extensions - Symmetry fixes for restore/cleanup during preset removal - Kimi's native skill mode and`ai_skills`Switch semantic alignment - A batch of discussions surrounding malformed config, non-dict init-options, and missing values.`SKILL.md`Boundary behavior reinforcement From the results, these issues weren't "single-point bug fixes," but rather a gradual process of bringing the entire skill chain back to a consistent state. --- ## A more important takeaway: We can't just fix by thread anymore After this experience, I have a very clear idea: **In the future, when handling these kinds of PRs, we can no longer passively fix them just by following the review thread.** We must first conduct a complete local audit, answering the following questions: - Are there multiple naming rules for the same concept? - Are create/restore/unregister/remove symmetrical? - Are the hook rendering and the actual artifacts consistent? - Are the local generation logic and the release artifacts consistent? - Do Python, bash, and PowerShell each maintain their own set of business rules? - Are the tests validating new behaviors or still locking old behaviors? If we don't conduct this audit first and instead directly follow existing comments to make changes, it's easy to fall into a state of local optima while the global behavior continues to drift. --- ## Discussion: What should be the goal of an architectural overhaul? Given the numerous implicit couplings in the current system, if we want it to converge in a more reasonable direction in the future, I believe the ideal form of the skill logic (personal opinion) should look like this: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph App["应用层 / 用例层"] InitSvc["InitService"] ExtSvc["ExtensionService"] PresetSvc["PresetService"] HookSvc["HookService"] ReleaseSvc["ReleaseBuildService"] end subgraph Domain["统一领域核心"] SkillSpec["SkillArtifact / SkillSpec"] Naming["SkillNamingPolicy"] Render["SkillRenderer"] Parse["SkillParser"] Validate["SkillInvariantValidator"] Registry["SkillRegistry / Ownership Model"] end subgraph Adapters["适配层"] FS["Filesystem Adapter"] GH["GitHub Release Builder"] Shell["Bash / PowerShell Wrapper"] end App --> Domain InitSvc --> SkillSpec ExtSvc --> SkillSpec PresetSvc --> SkillSpec HookSvc --> Naming ReleaseSvc --> SkillSpec SkillSpec --> Naming SkillSpec --> Render SkillSpec --> Parse SkillSpec --> Validate SkillSpec --> Registry Render --> FS ReleaseSvc --> GH Shell --> ReleaseSvc FS --> SkillFile["//SKILL.md"] GH --> ReleaseAsset["release asset / template zip"] ```The core idea behind this diagram can be summarized in one sentence: **A skill should first be a domain object, and only then a file.** In other words: - Naming conventions belong to...`SkillNamingPolicy`- Frontmatter/body rendering`SkillRenderer`- Parsing and verification`SkillParser` / `SkillInvariantValidator`- Ownership and source-of-truth`Registry`- The shell/PowerShell no longer implements business rules itself, but only acts as an adapter. After doing this,`SKILL.md`It's no longer the control plane itself, but merely a disk-based result of a domain object. --- ## Hypothetical Modification Comparison: The Difference in Feeling When Modifying a Rule ### Current State ```mermaid flowchart LR Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"] Rule --> A["init"] Rule --> B["preset"] Rule --> C["extension"] Rule --> D["hook"] Rule --> E["bash release"] Rule --> F["powershell release"] ```The immediate feeling is: **Changing one rule is like defusing six bombs at once.** ### The Ideal Post-Reform State ```mermaid flowchart LR Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"] Rule --> Core["Skill Domain Core"] Core --> A["init"] Core --> B["preset"] Core --> C["extension"] Core --> D["hook"] Core --> E["release builder"] ```The intuitive feeling is: **To change a rule, first change the core, then reuse the outer rules.** These two feelings are vastly different. The former is about "potential leaks everywhere"; The latter is the true goal—"default rules won't drift." --- ## Why this is particularly important for extension patterns After this review, a strong conclusion I've reached is: SpecKit is no longer just a scaffolding CLI; it's evolving into a platform with an extension ecosystem. Once platformized, the extension pattern will amplify the following problems: - Is skill naming globally consistent? - Is release artifacts consistent with local logic? - Does hook invocation consistently hit the actual artifact? - Is the overriding and restoration of presets/extensions symmetrical? - Are catalogs/priorities/ownerships explainable, auditable, and rollbackable? If these rules continue to be scattered across: - Python modules - Bash scripts - PowerShell scripts - File system conventions - Frontmatter metadata Then, with each additional agent and each new extension approach, the complexity will continue to rise. Conversely, if this domain layer is consolidated first, subsequent extension capabilities will be healthier. --- ## My Most Real Feelings This Time If I had to summarize this experience in a more subjective way, I would say this: **Under the current architecture, modifying skill-related code is like building roads on an old map;** **Only when the aforementioned domain modeling overhaul is completed will it feel more like adding rules to a real transportation system.** ** ** The former relies on experience, patches, and caution; The latter, in my opinion, is the correct model that truly supports the healthy development of the extension ecosystem. --- ## In Conclusion This fix made me realize something quite directly: **Many stressful reviews aren't because you can't fix bugs, but because the system itself hasn't properly consolidated its rules.** When business logic is sufficiently discrete, any change naturally amplifies the impact. At this point, reviews are no longer just about "finding errors," but more like reminding you: there are too many implicit couplings in this system. From the results, the problems were eventually fixed. But for me, the more valuable aspect is that through this debriefing process, I saw what truly needed fixing—not just the immediate bugs, but also the expressive capabilities required for the next stage of architecture design. If this personal discussion on "ideal architecture" can provide some reference for those maintaining complex CLIs, plugin systems, and workflow systems, then this effort was worthwhile.** --- ## Recommended Reading If you are interested in SpecKit or system refactoring evolution, we recommend reading other related articles on this site: - [SpecKit Structured Document-Driven Design and Practice (SDD)](/posts/sdd-series-part-2-spec-kit/) - [The Evolution of Complex CLIs: Refactoring Underlying Infrastructure Based on the Front-End](/posts/rust-core-npm-shell-cli-evolution/)
